title: Kefta skewers
subtitle: Moroccan spiced lamb mince skewers
excerpt: Kefta are Moroccan spiced lamb skewers: minced lamb mixed with onion, parsley, coriander, cumin, paprika, and cinnamon, then moulded around skewers and grilled until charred on the outside and still juicy inside. Simple to make, fast to cook, and the spice blend is what makes them.
sourceNotes:
• Paula Wolfert, *Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco* (1973). Principal reference for the spice proportions and the moulding technique.
• Claudia Roden, *The Food of Morocco* (2008). Cross-reference for the lamb-to-herb ratio and the onion grating method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Shape the kefta onto skewers, freeze on a lined tray until solid, then transfer to a bag. Cook from frozen under a hot grill, adding 3 to 4 minutes to the cooking time.
recipe.batchNotes: The mix doubles easily. Freeze half shaped onto skewers before cooking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The kefta mix can be made up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerated. Shape onto skewers just before cooking.

glossaryTerms.definition: Moroccan spiced minced meat (usually lamb or beef) moulded onto skewers and grilled. The spice blend of cumin, paprika, cinnamon, and fresh herbs is the defining character of the dish. Also spelled kofta in other parts of the Middle East and South Asia.

content.text: is one of the simplest things to make and one of the most satisfying. The meat mix takes 10 minutes to assemble, the skewers go onto a hot griddle for 8 minutes, and the whole thing is done. The spice blend is the point: cumin, paprika, and cinnamon together give a warmth that is distinctly Moroccan, different from the spicing of South Asian kofta or Middle Eastern versions.

Lamb mince with at least 15% fat gives the best result. Leaner mince holds less moisture and the kefta can dry out under the heat. Grating the onion rather than chopping it distributes moisture through the mix without leaving large pieces of onion that can cause the kefta to fall apart on the skewer.

items.prepNote: small, finely grated, excess liquid squeezed out

content.text: Metal skewers work better than wooden ones for kefta — they conduct heat to the centre and are reusable. If using wooden skewers, soak in water for 30 minutes first to prevent scorching. A ridged griddle pan gives good char marks and can be brought to a very high heat on the hob.

Combine {{lamb-mince}} of lamb mince in a large bowl with {{onion}} grated onion (squeezed dry), {{parsley-flat}} of chopped flat-leaf parsley, {{coriander}} of chopped coriander, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes if using, {{salt-table}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Mix thoroughly with your hands for 2 minutes until the mixture is cohesive. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm up.

Divide the mix into 8 equal portions. Take one portion and press it around a skewer, squeezing and rotating to form a sausage about 12 cm long and 3 cm wide. Smooth the surface with wet hands to prevent cracking. Repeat with the remaining portions. Chill for a further 10 minutes if the mix feels soft.

Heat a ridged griddle pan over the highest heat until smoking. Brush with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Cook the kefta for 3 to 4 minutes per side, turning twice, until charred on the outside and cooked through. Rest for 5 minutes, then serve with lemon wedges alongside flatbread, a salad, or both.

items.symptom: Kefta falls off the skewer during cooking
items.cause: The mix was too warm and soft, or the onion was not squeezed dry.
items.fix: Refrigerate the shaped kefta for at least 30 minutes before cooking. Cold fat holds the shape. Squeeze all excess liquid from the grated onion — wet mix does not bind properly.

items.cause: Lean mince was used, or the cooking time was too long.
items.fix: Use lamb mince with at least 15% fat. Cook to the minimum time — 3 to 4 minutes per side on a very hot griddle is sufficient. Overcooked kefta cannot be rescued.

items.cause: The griddle was not hot enough when the kefta went on.
items.fix: A ridged griddle pan needs 2 to 3 minutes on the highest heat before it can char properly. If the kefta sits and steams rather than sizzles immediately on contact, the pan is too cool.

content.text: Kefta is made across Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, as well as across the broader Middle East in related forms. In Morocco it is a street food staple, cooked over charcoal in market squares and served in flatbread with harissa and salad. The spicing differs from Turkish or South Asian equivalents primarily in the cinnamon, which is used more prominently in Moroccan cooking than in either of the other traditions. In Britain kefta became familiar through Moroccan restaurants in London and the growth of North African cooking in the 2000s and 2010s. It is now one of the easier ways to bring the flavour of that cuisine into a home kitchen.
